name: Monotonic Stack — Next Greater, Histogram, Trapping Rain

metadata.career_relevance: Monotonic stack is the highest-signal DSA pattern in senior SDE interviews — it separates O(n²) thinkers from O(n) thinkers. Largest Rectangle in Histogram (LeetCode #84) is a Flipkart/Amazon Hard. Trapping Rain Water appears at Google. Both are solvable in O(n) with a monotonic stack once you see the pattern.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Flipkart stock analysis: for each day, find next day with higher price
Naive O(n²): nested loop — TLE for n=100,000
Monotonic stack O(n): maintain decreasing stack of indices

Prices: [73,74,75,71,69,72,76,73]
Result: [ 1, 1, 4, 2, 1, 1, 0, 0] (days until warmer)

Key insight: when we see a higher price, it's the answer
for everything still in stack below us
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Next Greater Element, Daily Temperatures, Largest Rectangle, Trapping Rain Water — all O(n).

visual_aid.description: Monotonic stack trace for nextGreater([2,1,2,4,3]). Process each index: push 2(idx0). Push 1(idx1)—1<2 ok. See 2(idx2): 2>1(top) → pop idx1, result[1]=2. 2==2(top) → stop. Push idx2. See 4(idx3): 4>2(idx2)→pop,result[2]=4. 4>2(idx0)→pop,result[0]=4. Stack empty→push idx3. See 3(idx4): 3<4(top)→push. End. Stack has [idx3,idx4]: result stays -1.
visual_aid.alt_text: Monotonic stack trace for next greater element

step_by_step_solution.details: Maintain stack where heights are increasing. When we see a shorter bar: pop — the popped bar is the height of a rectangle. Width = current index - stack[-2] - 1 (or current index if stack empty). Area = height × width.

things_to_notice: Virtual 0 at end: forces all remaining bars to be popped and evaluated
Width calculation: i - stack.peek() - 1 (left boundary is prev stack element + 1)
If stack empty when computing width: left boundary is index 0, so width = i
O(n): each element pushed and popped at most once

industry_examples.use_case: Find the largest uniform-height transaction block in time-series data. Histogram pattern.

applications.interview_relevance: Largest Rectangle is canonical O(n) stack hard. Trapping Rain: two-pointer O(n) O(1) or stack O(n). Both appear in FAANG hard rounds.
applications.real_world_workflow: Monotonic stack triggers: 'next greater/smaller', 'previous greater/smaller', 'area under histogram', 'trapped water', 'span of stock price'. All O(n).

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Getting the width wrong in histogram. When popping bar at index `top`, if stack is empty: width = i (extends all the way to left). If stack not empty: width = i - stack.peek() - 1 (from stack top + 1 to i - 1).
common_gaps.remediation: Think of it as: the popped bar is the minimum height in some range. Left boundary = whatever's still on stack + 1. Right boundary = current index i - 1. Width = right - left + 1 = i - (stack.peek()+1) + 1 = i - stack.peek() - 1.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Histogram gives wrong area for bars that could extend to the left boundary

key_takeaways: Monotonic decreasing: pop when current > top → next greater element
Monotonic increasing: pop when current < top → largest area to the right
Histogram: increasing stack, pop when shorter, width=i-stack.peek()-1
Virtual sentinel 0 at end: flushes all remaining elements from stack
O(n): each index pushed and popped at most once — amortized O(1) per element
Store INDICES not values when you need width calculations

self_check_questions.question: Why is Largest Rectangle in Histogram O(n) with a monotonic stack, even though there's a nested while loop?
self_check_questions.answer: Amortized analysis: each bar index is pushed onto the stack exactly once and popped at most once. The while loop may pop multiple elements in one iteration, but those elements cannot be popped again. Over the entire array of n bars: total pushes = n, total pops ≤ n. So the total work across ALL iterations of the while loop is O(n), not O(n) per outer iteration. The nested structure is misleading — it's O(n) amortized, similar to ArrayList.add() being O(1) amortized despite occasional O(n) resize.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Queue Fundamentals — BFS traversal, implement stack with queues, circular buffer.
